sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Carpal tunnel syndrome is common in repetitive, forceful, or sustained wrist tasks. Screening should identify likely median nerve compression while not missing broader neuropathy or more serious neurological disease.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Numbness or tingling in thumb, index, middle, and radial ring finger
- Night waking, shaking the hand for relief, or dropping objects
- Thenar weakness or reduced pinch in more advanced cases
- Symptoms worsened by repetitive gripping, tool use, or prolonged wrist posture

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Progressive weakness, constant numbness, proximal neurological signs, or major functional loss raise urgency for medical review
- Symptoms can overlap with cervical radiculopathy, pronator syndrome, and generalized neuropathy
- Workstation and tool factors often contribute but do not fully explain every case

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen sensory territory, grip function, sleep disturbance, and task triggers
- Modify aggravating wrist positions and repetitive tasks where possible
- Escalate for persistent motor deficit or significant hand dysfunction

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Assess median nerve pattern, cervical overlap, and workplace exposure factors
- Use splinting discussion, tendon and nerve-friendly load modification, and progressive hand function work where appropriate
- Refer for medical review, nerve studies, or surgical opinion when weakness, atrophy, or refractory symptoms persist

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Clear documentation of sensory distribution and functional loss is more useful than a vague label of wrist pain
